moment_text: When I was first diagnosed with cancer, I had just turned 35. I had just finished nursing. My daughter and my world was, was flipped. It took a while to come to terms with what a diagnosis like that even meant not having mother or grandmother who had had a history of breast cancer. Not having the gene myself and not really being familiar with what. That journey could look like. It really changed things, but strangely, I found a piece that I, I didn't expect shortly after my surgeries and going through chemo, I actually. Found a peace with saying no. Um, in my life, for one of the first times, I am a people pleaser and have, have consistently tried to, to do all the things for, for everyone, for for good reasons. You know, I, I like to be helpful, I like to be a connector, but that really wore me down and it wasn't until. My cancer diagnosis and being so tired from chemo and feeling like I could finally say no without any guilt, without any regrets, and care for myself in the way that I needed to. I was surrounded by an extremely supportive family and and friend network. And so I think that that certainly helped. But, uh, saying no, uh. While I was recovering, really worked wonders for my own self love and, and self care, and it it, it helped me reconnect with myself. It helped me reconnect in my relationship with God, my intuition. And confidence in a way that I have fallen really far away from. And I, I think it, it actually opened me up to new opportunities. It, it made me kind of value my voice in a way that I, I didn't expect. And the clarity that, that came with rest and reflection and having clear boundaries helped me not only understand, but, but believe that I could do, um, hard things.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate with strong agency signals (setting_boundaries) and emotional signals (empowerment, relief) tied to a significant lived experience, making it eligible for pattern analysis.

moment_text: The other was actually maybe about two years post diagnosis and, and post getting back into my day-to-day life when I was in a job that I was doing very well at, but I was burning my own candle on both ends and got back into the, the patterns of doing everything for, for everyone all the time, and I completely lost myself again. I wasn't resting, I wasn't caring for my mind or my body. That had become so resilient after my diagnosis. And I was starting to experience some side effects, um, some physical side effects from just pushing too hard and, uh, was taking medication for those side effects. I was having neuropathy in my fingers and was taking a, a medication that. Helped with the neuropathy, but it also had some. Some mental impacts as well, and, and I think already being in a place of vulnerability, lacking of rest, not caring for myself, just put me in a spot where things got really dark really fast. And I, I remember a time, again, this is two years post treatment where I was curled up in the, the middle of the floor between my kids' rooms. Crying. And my son, he was only seven at the time, and my daughter was four. Um, my son came to me and he had to comfort me and tell me that everything was gonna be okay. And that was, that, that was a feeling of of rock bottom because I had gone from such a high, high, and understanding how important it was to care for myself Post-treatment too. Getting back into old patterns and having such a negative, um, self-talk and, and being so exhausted and not caring for myself that I was, I wasn't enough for myself. I was falling apart in front of my kids. And, and that was a really hard time. And, and that's where therapy actually came into my life because it was a moment where I was like, I can't, I, I can't do this on my own. And, and my husband helped me get to that point too, because he, he's. Very supportive, but he saw that I was falling apart and was like, let me help find someone for you to talk to, because this just, it's not working.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate with strong emotional signals (hopelessness, shame) and agency signals (seeking_support) within a family system interaction, making it eligible for pattern analysis.

moment_text: And then the final piece and kind of what connected us and, and got me on the path of positive outlook was. Menopause after I had a, a cancer recurrence. So with my, my recurrence life flipped on its head yet again, and I was forced to reevaluate everything that was going on in my life. And some of the, the side effects, the unknown side effects that medically induced menopause, uh, brings. And I was confused and I was learning as much as I could, as fast as I could, and talking to my doctors, listening to podcasts. Reading books and I was learning that with my physical side effects and with some of the mental side effects I was having. Again, I had to intentionally start to choose the life I wanted to lead in every area again. And so it kind of reminded me of that time, you know, post-cancer and, and figuring it out. What my boundaries were, what worked for me, what didn't, and how I wanted to move through the world. And, and so with the self-reflection that medically induced menopause required, that was a reconnection to my mental health and, and wellness. And. Prioritizing myself again in very specific and intentional ways.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate with clear barrier signals (lack_of_information) and agency signals (seeking_information) related to a specific health event (medically induced menopause), making it eligible for pattern analysis.
notes_for_reviewer: The life stage is tagged as menopause_transition because the moment is specifically about the onset of medically induced menopause, even though her chronological age is in the reproductive years.

moment_text: I can lose myself. I, I have found that I can lose myself if I don't have systems in place to reset and re and rest and, and recalibrate. And so with, with losing myself after my diagnosis, I was not taking time to. Rest at the end of the day and, and reflect on what I was proud of and, and what I had achieved. I was very much in a space where I was. Doing so much at work and, um, my, my job required that of me, uh, because I was kind of in a liaison role and so I was laser focused on my to-do list and making sure that I took care of this email and that I followed up with this, uh. Customer and, and that I, you know, was ready for the next meeting and I had the meeting notes, you know, ready for, for an upcoming presentation. And so at the end of the day, I would just focus on what I hadn't achieved and the fact that, you know, maybe I was really tired when it was bedtime with my kids and didn't put myself fully into it, or I was snippy with my husband. I would just go down kind of a negative rabbit hole just. Listing all the things that I didn't do, instead of celebrating what I was able to achieve and being proud of myself for that and building myself back up.
eligibility_rationale: The moment passes the evidence gate with clear emotional signals (self_doubt) and agency signals (self_blame) tied to a workplace system interaction, making it eligible for analysis of behavioral patterns.

moment_text: And so I think not having systems in place and, and, and not being mindful of how important it is to, to speak kindly to myself and be gracious, uh, with myself. Is why I fell back into some of the, the more negative patterns. Um, and, and I think with starting and restarting, taking a step back and. Realizing like, okay, this is not the path that, that I was headed down. Everything was great a couple years ago. Let me, let me go back to, you know, what worked well and, and reinstitute those things into my life is something that comes with grace and, and forgiveness and, and being like, okay, maybe I, I've. Let the pendulum swing too far in one direction, that's okay. I am not a horrible person for that. This is me talking to myself. I'm not a horrible person for that. Let me go back to, to what worked. And so, um, being okay with, with knowing that life is gonna ebb and flow and change, but making those adjustments and, and, and being okay with that, uh, makes a a difference.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals ('making_decisions') as the speaker describes the internal process of deciding to return to healthier patterns.

moment_text: You know, I think women in general do it to themselves. I hear so much. I was literally walking to go have my coffee and write in my journal this morning and I'm going to Peru and I wanna set my intentions for what I wanna experience. And one of the things I'm thinking about is. I'm a very positive person, but I go into things and I think about what could go wrong. And so it is, so, it is natural to us. I'm sharing that to say that this is very natural for women. So instead of saying what could go right or what do we do, that was good and acknowledging that. And so I think it's great that you are recognizing the need to not only give yourself grace, but to celebrate your wins big and small at every stage.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains emotional signals ('validation', 'connection') and a system interaction ('peer_community') as the host validates the guest's experience by sharing her own.

moment_text: Yeah, I, I think it's exactly what you said. It's, it's not always easy. It's, it's recognizing that life is going to change and, and for me, I can be an emotional rollercoaster on most days, but what I have noticed for myself is when I'm operating. At my highest self, taking my own advice as, as Beyonce would say, it's, um, for, for me, it's taking early morning walks. I'm not a morning person, but I have realized that the stillness of the mourning and the. Time that I have to wake up and do a, um, my morning gratitude journaling the time that I have to, to pray, the time that I have to reflect on what it is I wanna achieve during the day. It's so grounding. And so when I carve out that time and I'm consistent about my morning routine, it makes a huge difference.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals ('tracking_symptoms', 'making_decisions') as the speaker describes the specific, intentional actions she takes for her mental wellness.

moment_text: And another thing that I've noticed and and that helps me march towards my mental wellness and, and really operate at my highest level is, is kind of like what I, I mentioned earlier. I've really made it a habit to. Do nighttime, um, reflections in, in journaling. I, I have three prompts, or no, four prompts actually, that help me get into a more calm and relaxed mind, uh, state before going to bed. And it's writing down what I love about myself for that day, what I'm proud of myself for that day, what I forgive myself for. And then what held me back and what change I can make in the day to come. And so that nighttime practice is just as important as the morning grounding because that's where I can carve out space for grace and love and just a reminder that everything is ultimately going to be okay. And if I'm blessed enough to wake up in the, you know, for the next day, there are, you know, adjustments that I can make because I've reflected on it the night before in a, in a kind way, um, for myself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains agency signals ('tracking_symptoms', 'making_decisions') as the speaker details a specific, structured practice she developed to support her mental health.

moment_text: Positive Outlook is actually. A blog that I created, um, shortly after my breast cancer recurrence. Um, just for a a little bit of background, I had hormone sensitive, um, invasive ductal carcinoma. Um, and so that essentially means that the, the type of breast cancer that I had thrived and continued to grow in an environment where my body was naturally producing. Estrogen and progesterone, and so there is anti-hormone therapy essentially that I've been on in order to. Reduce the risk of another, uh, recurrence. And that anti-hormone therapy is ovarian suppression. So, uh, every month I have a shot to prevent my ovaries from, um, producing estrogen and progesterone. And then I also take an aromatase inhibitor that, uh, prevents my body fat cells, my liver, all of the, my kidneys. All, all of the things that, uh, produce trace amounts of, uh, estrogen are essentially. No longer doing that because the aromatase Inha inhibitors, and so that brings on. Very severe menopausal symptoms, especially when going from a, a place of always having an, a normal cycle, um, or predictable cycle, I'll say, um, for my whole life to not having anything at all. And so I, I was experiencing extreme hot flashes. I was experiencing dizziness, um, vertigo essentially, where it got to the point where I couldn't even drive, uh, because I was so dizzy. I was having heart palpitations. I was having, uh, joint pain, extreme brain fog, and all of those symptoms were a surprise to me because I, I thought that menopause, when I started the process was just gonna be a couple of hot flashes here and there, and I was, I was so wrong and I was confused and, um, I, I didn't really know what to do about that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong barrier signals ('lack_of_information', 'education_gap') and emotional signals ('confusion', 'overwhelm') within a healthcare provider context, highlighting a significant knowledge gap about treatment side effects.
notes_for_reviewer: This is a powerful lived experience of medically-induced menopause and the shock of its symptoms, which could be a key insight about patient education and preparedness.

moment_text: I was. Struggling, um, with survivor's guilt and, and pushing for, for more support. And I was, I was struggling with, you know, understanding, um, what it meant for my long-term health. And so I started reading, uh, a lot of, uh, books. I started listening to seminars. And as I started to amass more and more, um, knowledge about what the menopause journey looks like, I got to a point where I just had to journal. And get some of my thoughts out and try and create a space for sharing stories and, uh, using shared stories to, to help with my own journey and, and to potentially help other women.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong agency signals ('seeking_information', 'sharing_story') and barrier signals ('lack_of_support') as the speaker describes her proactive response to a gap in her care experience.

moment_text: So, so I created positive outlook. Essentially as, as an outlet for my own healing. But it has since evolved into a platform and, and continues to evolve, um, into a platform where I. Talk about the importance of early detection, because early detection saved my life twice. And then I also use it as a space to share some of the menopause resources that I'm learning about from a product standpoint. From a. Helpful resources standpoint, you know, which doctors are putting out great information, which telehealth platforms are helpful, which books, um, you know, opened my eyes, so sharing those resources. And then I also use it as a space for, uh, creating community. And so about once or twice a year, I host events, intimate events that are called the second bloom, so we can talk about this new phase of life. And in those events, I invite women of all journeys. So whether they're perimenopause, whether they are postmenopausal, or just interested in, in learning more. Uh, those events come together in, in places and spaces where women are, are doing the things that they wouldn't. Love to do in general. So shopping, having brunch, because our, our time is limited and, and having sisterhood and community in, in fun spaces is it's life giving to me. And so I hope it's the same for other women.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong agency signals ('sharing_story') and emotional signals ('empowerment', 'connection') within a peer community context, detailing the creation of a support system.

moment_text: And, and so when we come together in those, um, less formal but fun spaces, we're also carving out time to talk about the menopause journey and. What it can look like and what some solutions are. And so I feel like that community aspect is really important because I thought that I was alone in my journey when I first started, but I have since realized that I'm not close to being alone. And that feels really good.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong emotional signals ('isolation', 'relief', 'belonging') and a barrier signal ('social_isolation') within a peer community context, directly stating the impact of community on overcoming isolation.

moment_text: I think in the community events that, um, I, I host a lot of my, um, friends and guests are able to. Put their learnings to action by scheduling appointments with the panelists who I bring in and, um, by, you know, being able to try out products that may or may not, you know, work for them in, in the moment.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: This moment contains strong agency signals ('seeking_care', 'making_decisions') and describes how a peer community interaction directly leads to healthcare engagement.

moment_text: But it, it kind of gives them a sense of ownership and advocacy for themselves and, and what they're going through. And so positive outlook is, it has. Grown, you know, past being my own personal blog and, and healing space to a, a space of, you know, community and, and education. And so, I, I, it, it brings me joy. It, it kind of, uh, supports my mental health as well. Um, and it's my hope that it supports the, the menopause and the, you know, mental journey for all of the, uh, women who are touched by it.
eligibility_rationale: This moment describes a protective factor (community building) and contains clear agency and emotional signals within a peer community interaction, meeting the A2 evidence gate.

moment_text: I think it's fantastic. You started it as a blog, which gave you voice, which gave you agency, which was self-care, because giving voice to your experience is very cathartic, so that's fantastic. I applaud you so much, but I applaud more the recognizing and letting it sort of organically unfold. Based on the circumstances that you come across during your experience and it's, I think it's interesting as well the niche, a little bit of it, the, the coming out and it being around menopause, but having had medically induced menopause, that's a very. Different audience. Yeah. Sensitivities there. You know, all women are gonna go through menopause, but when you are forced into menopause because of an illness, a, you know, medication, whatever it may be, that change that, that is upending because you're not expecting it. Whereas, you know, you do expect menopause when you reach certain age and certain milestones.
eligibility_rationale: This moment provides validation and contains emotional and agency signals within a peer community context, meeting the A2 evidence gate.

moment_text: From a survivorship standpoint, there are still gaps in in care. And one of the things that was wonderful and and very helpful to me with my diagnosis is even though I felt like there was so much information coming at me so fast, and it was almost like having to learn a new language with the words to describe what I was going through, understanding the different procedures, all of those things. There is support built in after a diagnosis where most medical facilities have nurse navigators who help us make appointments with different providers, with, you know, the breast surgeon, with the plastics, with a nutritionist, but to, to make it a little bit easier, um, for the patient to just know where to go. But what I have personally found is post. Treatment, post active hands-on treatment, there are fewer resources for survivors. I felt like when I rang the bell, it was such significant experience, but I was released into a world that I didn't quite know how to navigate, and I felt like. With the menopausal symptoms that I was experiencing, my lack of, of knowledge of what those look like. I was putting together puzzle pieces, but not just puzzle pieces. It was almost like someone had dumped a box of puzzle pieces. On me. I didn't have the top of what that puzzle looked like, and I didn't know what was survivorship, what was menopause, what was just, you know, uh, mourning the future that I thought that I was gonna have. And I didn't know how many pieces there even were to that puzzle.
eligibility_rationale: This moment is a strong barrier encounter with multiple barrier signals (fragmented_care, lack_of_support) and emotional signals (overwhelm, confusion) within a healthcare provider interaction, meeting the A2 evidence gate.

moment_text: I really feel like there's a gap in care for younger survivors, and so inserting ourselves into the menopause conversation and saying, Hey, look, we're not only dealing with extreme symptoms in the immediate term, but we're also at a higher risk. Some of the long-term health changes that happen with menopause. So we're at higher risk for heart disease, higher risk for osteoporosis, higher risk for some of the. Mental changes, cognitive changes that, that happen with medically induced menopause. So we're being fast forwarded into kind of unknown territory and, and new health, um, issues, but there isn't as much support as, as we need within that space.
eligibility_rationale: This moment clearly identifies multiple systemic barrier signals (fragmented_care, lack_of_support) within a healthcare provider context, meeting the A2 evidence gate.

moment_text: So I do want to use positive outlook as a platform to, to bring attention to, to that element as well, that, um, survivorship is. The greatest gift, but I, I do think that quality of life, and that means in the immediate term and the long term is also something that survivors should be able to aspire to and not feel guilty about it. Because I struggled with a little bit of survivor's guilt as well in, in trying to advocate for myself.
eligibility_rationale: This moment contains strong agency signals (self_advocacy), emotional signals (shame), and a barrier signal (stigma), meeting the A2 evidence gate.
notes_for_reviewer: The mention of 'survivor's guilt' is tagged as both shame (emotional signal) and stigma (barrier signal) as it represents an internalized stigma that creates an emotional barrier to self-advocacy.

moment_text: I do imagine it moving more or envision it moving more into an advocacy space. Um, so holding on to early detection and the importance of that, but really shifting into intentional advocacy for survivors like myself for survivors, uh, like my daughter and. Making sure that there is care and attention to this, this journey, and that women are being informed of what's to come. And through that information can, can make better choices about, uh, their own healthcare. I, I know that they're. It is some great nonpartisan work and energy behind increased menopause education. And that's, that's very important, uh, to me because it, it means that I'm not walking into a doctor's office and trying to explain what's going on and asking, should I talk to a cardiologist? Should I talk to a neurologist? I, I think if there's more. Information and, and more education from a a physician standpoint, some of those choices will be clearer, uh, for survivors and, and I think for women who have a, a family history of, of breast cancer and may want to have a prophylactic ectomy or, you know, double mastectomy or something of, of that sort, if they understand. What comes along with those changes, then, then they can just make, uh, better choices for themselves. So I, I think connecting the dots and being an advocate in the space, that quality of life really matters in the, the cancer community is important. And so using positive outlook as a space for doing that is. Is is very motivating and, and it excites me.
eligibility_rationale: This moment details an advocacy goal aimed at addressing specific barriers (lack_of_information, training_gap) with clear agency signals (self_advocacy), meeting the A2 evidence gate.

moment_text: Two things. I wanna leave them with two things. Uh, first is that no experience is wasted in a way that, um. Not promoting toxic positivity or anything like that, but I, I have learned that even some of the hard times that I've had to endure have been. You know, the opportunities for growth. I didn't think that while going through it, but being able to reflect back and, and having the privilege of, of reflecting back, I've been able to use the, the tough times. My own, you know, cancer journey, my own mental health struggles as a place of learning and. I just want to remind everyone that our paths are ever evolving and we're exposed to people and situations at different points in our life, and I, I think holding onto to those experiences and encounters can ultimately you.
eligibility_rationale: This moment describes a protective factor and coping mechanism. It contains emotional and agency signals, and while system_interaction is 'none', the presence of these signals makes it eligible per rule A2.

moment_text: Serve us all in, in the future and, and contribute to the, the greater good. So, so no experience is, is wasted and, and we can all use them to our, our power and, and our highest good. So that's one thing to, to just remember that even the hard times, um, can, you know, be a, a blessing to someone else or, or even to ourselves.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has agency signals (sharing_story) and a non-none system interaction (peer_community).

moment_text: The last thing I, I wouldn't be an advocate if I didn't say this, but get a self or do a self breast exam. Know your normal. It is so important to understand that our bodies are changing, uh, at, at all times. And when we have a good grasp of what's normal for, um, ourselves, then we can, we can make choices to, to care for ourselves.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has strong agency signals (self_advocacy, seeking_information, tracking_symptoms).

moment_text: Self breast exams can be a little bit. Intimidating if, if you're not familiar with how to do one, but it's super easy. The best time to do a self breast exam is three days after your cycle ends because our breasts go through, uh, different changes throughout the month. And so that's going to be the most normal baseline from a hormonal standpoint. So. Three days after your cycle ends is a great time to do a self-breast exam. If you have hit menopause and are no longer having a cycle, just choose the same day every month, uh, to do a self-breast exam and notice any, any changes there. Um, because when we, when know what's normal for us, like I said, we can make choices.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has both barrier signals (lack_of_information, education_gap) and agency signals (seeking_information).
notes_for_reviewer: This moment directly addresses an education gap by providing specific, actionable instructions for different life stages (menstruating and menopausal).

moment_text: Set up appointments to get checked out, and early detection really does, um, save, save lives. Um, I had stage one c invasive breast cancer and, um, was able to get treatment and do things that maybe I wouldn't have, uh, if, if I didn't do that chance self breast exam. So, so get in there and, and get comfortable with yourself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: has strong agency signals (seeking_care, self_advocacy) and a direct system interaction (healthcare_provider).
notes_for_reviewer: Connects the practical advice from the previous moment to a real-life outcome, emphasizing the importance of early detection.
